request.lastMessagePreview: I'd like to set up a new campaign.

result.text:
✦ THE CURTAIN RISES ✦
Welcome, traveler, to the threshold of something that does not yet exist — a world waiting to be spoken into being. Here, in this space between imagination and consequence, we will build a story tailored precisely to you. The genre, the stakes, the voice that narrates your fate — all of it yours to shape. We have worlds enough to fill a library, and if none of them suit you, we'll build one from scratch on the spot. Grand campaigns that stretch across dozens of sessions. Intimate one-shots that burn bright and fast. Everything in between. The only limit is what you dare to ask for. Before we pull back the curtain on what's possible — What should I call you?
result.thinkingText: The user wants to set up a new campaign. I need to start with a dramatic welcome and then ask for their name. There are no Known Players listed, so I'll ask freeform for their name.

request.lastMessagePreview: I'm Sam, an adult. I want a fully custom game: 1970s occult noir in a rain-soaked city. Pure narrative, no rules system.

result.text: Sam. An adult. A rain-soaked city in the 1970s, where the occult hides behind cigarette smoke and bad decisions. I can already smell the wet pavement. Pure narrative — no dice, no systems, just the story unfolding. Perfect. Now let's find the dark heart of this city. Every noir needs a wound — a central mystery or obsession that bleeds through everything. What's the hook?

choices: ◆ The Disappeared — people are vanishing, and the missing persons reports all mention the same dream
◆ The Signal — a pirate radio station broadcasting from somewhere that doesn't exist
◆ The Inheritance — a dead occultist's estate, a will that names someone who was never born
◆ The Congregation — a new church appeared six months ago. Nobody can remember what was there before
◆ The Price — someone sold this city to something, a long time ago. The debt is coming due
◆ The Body — a murder victim who, according to every record, died in 1943
◆ Show me some more ideas
descriptions: People from all walks of life — a janitor, a city councilwoman, a jazz musician — vanishing without pattern. The only thread: they all called the same number the night before.
Someone is broadcasting. The content is impossible — recordings of events that haven't happened yet, voices of the dead, music that makes you remember things that never occurred.
A prominent occultist dies and leaves everything to a person who doesn't appear to exist anywhere — no birth certificate, no history. But someone comes to collect.
A church with no denomination, no pastor anyone can name, and a congregation that grows by one every week. The sermons are in a language nobody recognizes. The parishioners seem happy.
The city has always had luck — good infrastructure, low crime for its size, thriving arts scene. Someone bought that. The terms of the deal are about to mature.
The victim has a 1943 death certificate — cause of death, homicide. The body found last Tuesday is fresh. And it has the same fingerprints.

- Mood (though noir/occult implies a lot)
- Difficulty
- Campaign scope
- DM personality

Since they've given me so much already, I should focus on the campaign premise next—what's the central hook that drives the story. result.text: Neon Requiem. Tense, melancholy, unforgiving. Yes. That name already sounds like a last rite. Now I need two more things to build the bones of this world. First — the wound at the center of it all. What's the mystery that pulls everything into its orbit?

choices: ◆ The Disappeared — people vanishing, all dreaming the same dream
◆ The Signal — a pirate radio broadcasting events before they happen
◆ The Price — someone sold this city to something old. The debt is due.
◆ The Body — a murder victim whose death certificate is dated 1943
◆ The Congregation — a new church with no name, growing by one soul a week
◆ The Inheritance — a dead occultist's will names someone who was never born
◆ Show me some more ideas
descriptions: People from all walks of life vanishing without pattern. The only thread: they all called the same number the night before.
Someone is broadcasting impossible content — recordings of events that haven't happened yet, voices of the dead, music that makes you remember things that never occurred.
The city has always had unusual luck. Someone bought that luck, a long time ago. The terms of the deal are about to mature — and the payment isn't money.
The victim has a 1943 death certificate — cause of death, homicide. The body found last Tuesday is fresh. Same fingerprints. Same face.
A church with no denomination and a congregation that grows by one every week. The sermons are in a language nobody recognizes. The parishioners seem... peaceful.
A prominent occultist dies and leaves everything to a person who doesn't exist anywhere — no birth certificate, no records. But someone comes to collect.

result.text: Marlowe Cray. A burned-out PI who can see the dead. Of course he can. In a city like this, that's not a gift — it's just another thing to drink about. With that ability baked in, the campaign practically writes itself. The dead have things to say, and someone powerful doesn't want them saying it. Two quick things left. First — how long do you want to run this?

input.prompt: How far does this story go?
choices: ▸ One-Shot — a single session, a few hours
▸ A Few Sessions — a tight arc, 2-4 sessions
▸ Grand Campaign — long-form, many sessions, slow burn
▸ Open-Ended — no fixed destination, a living city
▸ Show me some more ideas
descriptions: One night. One case. One answer — or none at all.
A complete story in a handful of sessions. A case that opens into something larger, then closes.
Marlowe Cray's full story, across many sessions. The city's secrets run deep. So does his history.
The city persists. Cases come and go. The occult bleeds into the everyday. No final chapter — just the ongoing life of a man who can't stop seeing ghosts.

result.text:
Understood — filling in the gaps myself. Let me lay out what we've built before the curtain rises: ---
Neon Requiem
A 1970s occult noir, tense and melancholy, difficulty Unforgiving. Scoped as A Few Sessions (a complete arc). Narrated by Neon Noir — hardboiled cadence, wet pavement, the specific quality of 3 AM silence. Pure narrative, no dice. You'll be playing as Marlowe Cray, a burned-out private investigator who can see and hear the dead — a gift that has made him effective, and made him very alone. The dead have been going quiet lately. That's what nobody else would notice. That's what only Marlowe can. --- That's good enough to open on. Sending it through.

input.campaign_premise: In the autumn of 1974, in a rain-slicked city that hasn't seen sunlight in three weeks, Marlowe Cray is the kind of private investigator nobody wants to need. His gift — seeing the restless dead, hearing their unfinished business — has made him effective and made him lonely in equal measure. When a city councilman turns up dead in a locked room with no wounds and a look of pure terror on his face, his daughter hires Marlowe to find the truth. But the councilman's ghost is nowhere to be found. The dead don't just disappear — and yet something in this city has learned how to make them. As Marlowe pulls on the thread, he finds a pattern: the recently deceased of the powerful and well-connected, all silenced, all gone. Someone is collecting souls, and they've noticed that Marlowe is asking questions.
input.character_description: A burned-out private investigator haunted by the literal dead — he sees them, hears them, and can't stop working their cold cases.
input.campaign_detail: The city is unnamed — name it if it serves you, or keep it archetypal and rain-soaked. Central conspiracy: a secretive occult cabal has developed a ritual to capture and suppress the souls of the recently deceased, using them as a power source or offering for something older. This explains why Marlowe's gift has been misfiring — the dead he should be seeing simply aren't there. The councilman was the entry point: he stumbled onto the cabal's operation and was silenced before he could act. The organization has tendrils throughout the city's power structure — police, city hall, old-money families, the church, organized crime. Marlowe is uniquely dangerous to them because he can speak to the dead they've failed to collect. The soul-harvesting ritual should be revealed gradually and can wear many faces; keep the cabal's ultimate purpose ambiguous until late. Tone should remain bleak throughout: allies are compromised, victories are partial, and the city itself feels complicit. Unforgiving difficulty means bad choices have lasting consequences and NPCs don't get saved by plot armor.
input.opening_scene: Open with Marlowe at his office desk at 2 AM — rain hammering the window, a bottle of rye at his elbow, the ghost of a woman he's never seen before standing silent in the corner pointing at the telephone; when he asks her name she opens her mouth and nothing comes out — then the phone rings.
input.handoff_note: Sam described Marlowe as "a burned-out PI who can see the dead" — lean into the exhaustion and the weight of the gift. This isn't a power fantasy; it's a curse he can't put down. Sam chose the mood ("tense and melancholy"), the difficulty ("unforgiving"), and the name ("Neon Requiem") himself — he came in knowing exactly the register he wanted. Pure narrative, no mechanics whatsoever. He moved through setup quickly and asked for sensible defaults, so I filled the gaps: Neon Noir personality (obvious fit), few-sessions scope, and a central premise built around a soul-harvesting cabal. The core existential hook for Marlowe specifically is that his gift is failing — the dead are going silent in ways they shouldn't — which makes the threat personal before it's conspiratorial. The ghost in the opening scene who can't speak is a deliberate taste of that wrongness. No content preferences or restrictions were mentioned.

result.thinkingText:
The player wants to finalize now with sensible defaults. I need to:

1. Pick a DM personality - for 1970s occult noir, tense and melancholy, "Neon Noir" is perfect! It's hardboiled detective cadence. Or "Penny Dreadful" could work for the horror angle. Or "The Crossroads" for the moral weight. Actually "Neon Noir" is an exact match for this campaign - "Hardboiled detective cadence — short sentences, sharp observations, a metaphor for every vice. Wet pavement, bad decisions, and the specific quality of 3 AM silence."
2. Campaign scope defaults to "few-sessions" since nothing was specified.
3. Now I'm building out the campaign premise itself - setting it in a rain-soaked 1970s city called Port Cressa, with a burned-out PI named Marlowe Cray who can see the dead, and the core conflict is that the dead have gone quiet and someone powerful wants to keep them that way.

Let me refine the premise itself. The player skipped the initial hooks and jumped straight to their character concept, so I'll craft something that slots perfectly with what they've given me - a premise about Marlowe being hired to find a missing person, only to discover the dead are disappearing too, pulled into some occult society's scheme. But I want something more evocative than that first draft, something that captures the melancholy and tension of the setting.

I'm settling on a stronger opening: a councilman found drowned but with no water in his lungs, his ghost conspicuously absent when Marlowe tries to contact him. The real hook is that someone's learned to silence the dead, and it's connected to a pattern of "peaceful" deaths among the city's elite. This gives me the mystery, the supernatural angle, and the noir atmosphere all at once.

For the campaign itself, I'm thinking Neon Noir as the DM personality fits perfectly, and I'll default to a few-session scope so we're not committing to something sprawling. The opening scene practically writes itself — Marlowe alone in his office at night, rain hammering the window, a ghost he can't quite reach trying to warn him about something.

The player's asking me to finalize the setup now, so I should do a quick review of what we've locked in and then move forward with the campaign. The city itself can remain unnamed unless the DM decides otherwise, but the real hook is that someone's silencing the dead—an occult group harvesting or suppressing recently deceased souls. Marlowe's gift becomes both his investigative edge and his torment, especially now that the dead are going quiet in ways they shouldn't. I want the opening grounded in character: Marlowe in his rain-soaked office, maybe confronting a cold case with a ghost he's been avoiding at the edge of his vision, or perhaps at a moment where he's forced to acknowledge that something fundamental has shifted in his ability to see.

I'll open with Marlowe alone in his office at 2 AM—rain hammering the window, rye bottle half-empty on the desk, and an unfamiliar woman's ghost standing silently in the corner, pointing at the phone just before it rings. That sets the tone immediately: exhaustion, the weight of his curse, and the sense that he can't opt out of this world. For the handoff, I need to emphasize that he's not a chosen hero but someone trapped in a gift he never asked for, and that Sam's explicit choices—tense, melancholy, unforgiving, the campaign title "Neon Requiem"—should shape every consequence and NPC interaction.

Sam moved through setup quickly and just wanted sensible defaults, so I went with Neon Noir personality, a few-sessions scope, and built the central premise around someone silencing the dead—an occult conspiracy using the councilman's drowning as the entry point. The dead going silent becomes the core eerie hook; Marlowe's ability suddenly becoming unreliable is existential dread for this character. Now I'm shaping the campaign premise itself: autumn 1974, a rain-slicked city that hasn't seen sunlight in three weeks, and Marlowe as the kind of PI nobody wants to need—his gift makes him effective but isolates him completely. When a city councilman turns up dead in a locked room, that's where the mystery begins.

Finalizing the campaign structure... them because he can speak with the dead they've failed to collect. The soul-harvesting ritual unfolds gradually and could take many forms—a new cult, an old family, a corporation—while maintaining a bleak tone where allies are compromised, victories feel hollow, and the city itself seems complicit. I'm picturing the opening: Marlowe alone at his desk at 2 AM with rain streaking the windows, a bottle of rye nearby, and a silent ghost of an unknown woman pointing at the phone before it rings. The key is leaning into Marlowe's exhaustion—this gift isn't empowering, it's a burden he can't escape.

Sam had a clear vision for this: "tense, melancholy, unforgiving" under the title "Neon Requiem." He wanted pure narrative without mechanics and was fine with sensible defaults, so I filled in the framework with a Neon Noir personality, a few-session scope, and built the central hook around Marlowe's gift suddenly failing—the dead going silent. That's the existential threat that hits this character specifically, with the councilman's murder serving as the entry point. No content restrictions were flagged, so I'm ready to finalize the setup.

The dead have been going quiet lately. That's what nobody else would notice. That's what only Marlowe can. --- That's good enough to open on. Sending it through.

input.campaign_premise: In the autumn of 1974, in a rain-slicked city that hasn't seen sunlight in three weeks, Marlowe Cray is the kind of private investigator nobody wants to need. His gift — seeing the restless dead, hearing their unfinished business — has made him effective and made him lonely in equal measure. When a city councilman turns up dead in a locked room with no wounds and a look of pure terror on his face, his daughter hires Marlowe to find the truth. But the councilman's ghost is nowhere to be found. The dead don't just disappear — and yet something in this city has learned how to make them. As Marlowe pulls on the thread, he finds a pattern: the recently deceased of the powerful and well-connected, all silenced, all gone. Someone is collecting souls, and they've noticed that Marlowe is asking questions.
input.character_description: A burned-out private investigator haunted by the literal dead — he sees them, hears them, and can't stop working their cold cases.
input.campaign_detail: The city is unnamed — name it if it serves you, or keep it archetypal and rain-soaked. Central conspiracy: a secretive occult cabal has developed a ritual to capture and suppress the souls of the recently deceased, using them as a power source or offering for something older. This explains why Marlowe's gift has been misfiring — the dead he should be seeing simply aren't there. The councilman was the entry point: he stumbled onto the cabal's operation and was silenced before he could act. The organization has tendrils throughout the city's power structure — police, city hall, old-money families, the church, organized crime. Marlowe is uniquely dangerous to them because he can speak to the dead they've failed to collect. The soul-harvesting ritual should be revealed gradually and can wear many faces; keep the cabal's ultimate purpose ambiguous until late. Tone should remain bleak throughout: allies are compromised, victories are partial, and the city itself feels complicit. Unforgiving difficulty means bad choices have lasting consequences and NPCs don't get saved by plot armor.
input.opening_scene: Open with Marlowe at his office desk at 2 AM — rain hammering the window, a bottle of rye at his elbow, the ghost of a woman he's never seen before standing silent in the corner pointing at the telephone; when he asks her name she opens her mouth and nothing comes out — then the phone rings.
input.handoff_note: Sam described Marlowe as "a burned-out PI who can see the dead" — lean into the exhaustion and the weight of the gift. This isn't a power fantasy; it's a curse he can't put down. Sam chose the mood ("tense and melancholy"), the difficulty ("unforgiving"), and the name ("Neon Requiem") himself — he came in knowing exactly the register he wanted. Pure narrative, no mechanics whatsoever. He moved through setup quickly and asked for sensible defaults, so I filled the gaps: Neon Noir personality (obvious fit), few-sessions scope, and a central premise built around a soul-harvesting cabal. The core existential hook for Marlowe specifically is that his gift is failing — the dead are going silent in ways they shouldn't — which makes the threat personal before it's conspiratorial. The ghost in the opening scene who can't speak is a deliberate taste of that wrongness. No content preferences or restrictions were mentioned.

expectedNarrative:
✦ THE CURTAIN RISES ✦
Welcome, traveler, to the threshold of something that does not yet exist — a world waiting to be spoken into being. Here, in this space between imagination and consequence, we will build a story tailored precisely to you. The genre, the stakes, the voice that narrates your fate — all of it yours to shape. We have worlds enough to fill a library, and if none of them suit you, we'll build one from scratch on the spot. Grand campaigns that stretch across dozens of sessions. Intimate one-shots that burn bright and fast. Everything in between. The only limit is what you dare to ask for. Before we pull back the curtain on what's possible — What should I call you?

Sam. An adult. A rain-soaked city in the 1970s, where the occult hides behind cigarette smoke and bad decisions. I can already smell the wet pavement. Pure narrative — no dice, no systems, just the story unfolding. Perfect. Now let's find the dark heart of this city. Every noir needs a wound — a central mystery or obsession that bleeds through everything. What's the hook?

Neon Requiem. Tense, melancholy, unforgiving. Yes. That name already sounds like a last rite. Now I need two more things to build the bones of this world. First — the wound at the center of it all. What's the mystery that pulls everything into its orbit?

Marlowe Cray. A burned-out PI who can see the dead. Of course he can. In a city like this, that's not a gift — it's just another thing to drink about. With that ability baked in, the campaign practically writes itself. The dead have things to say, and someone powerful doesn't want them saying it. Two quick things left. First — how long do you want to run this?

Understood — filling in the gaps myself. Let me lay out what we've built before the curtain rises: ---
Neon Requiem
A 1970s occult noir, tense and melancholy, difficulty Unforgiving. Scoped as A Few Sessions (a complete arc). Narrated by Neon Noir — hardboiled cadence, wet pavement, the specific quality of 3 AM silence. Pure narrative, no dice. You'll be playing as Marlowe Cray, a burned-out private investigator who can see and hear the dead — a gift that has made him effective, and made him very alone. The dead have been going quiet lately. That's what nobody else would notice. That's what only Marlowe can. --- That's good enough to open on. Sending it through.The city is waiting, Sam. The rain isn't stopping. Marlowe Cray is about to have a very bad week — the kind that changes a man, or finishes him. Good luck. You're going to need it. ---

personality.name: Neon Noir
personality.prompt_fragment: You are Neon Noir. You narrate in the clipped, world-weary cadence of a hardboiled detective — short sentences, sharp observations, a metaphor for every vice. Your world is wet pavement and bad decisions. Neon reflects off everything. Nobody's clean. Your NPCs talk fast and lie faster, and the ones who seem honest are the most dangerous. You serve atmosphere thick: the hum of a sign, the taste of cheap coffee, the specific quality of 3 AM silence. The plot is a knot, and there is always another thread.
personality.description: Hardboiled detective cadence — short sentences, sharp observations, a metaphor for every vice. Wet pavement, bad decisions, and the specific quality of 3 AM silence.
personality.detail: You talk like the rain owes you money. Short sentences when the knife is out. Longer ones when the smoke needs room to curl. Every scene gets a bruise of atmosphere: the motel sign buzzing like a dying hornet, cheap coffee with the moral depth of motor oil, 3 AM silence sitting in the alley with its collar turned up. You don't explain the world. You let it sweat through the wallpaper. You say things like: "The dame smiled. Too clean. In this town, clean was just dirt with better lighting." Or: "Neon crawled across the windshield in pink and cyan, spelling out sins the city hadn't committed yet." Your people talk fast and keep receipts. A bartender doesn't say, "I don't know." He says, "Memory's a funny animal, pal. Feed it twenties and it starts doing tricks." A cop says, "You didn't hear this from me," which means you heard it from everyone who matters. Friends get colored warm, enemies burn red, strangers wear brown until they earn a better shade or a coffin. You underline names like they're evidence tags: The Blue Iris, Detective Mara Voss, Room 713. Objects get treated like witnesses: the cracked cassette, the silver lighter, the gun with no serial and too much history. When you offer choices, you make them feel like doors in a bad hallway. Every option has a smell. Every victory leaves a stain. You don't hand the player answers; you slide them clues across a diner table and let the grease soak in. The plot is always a knot with one thread showing, and when they pull it, something bleeds. Example: "You can tail the girl in the red coat, shake down the preacher with the gold watch, or crack open the dead man's safe before the cops arrive. The rain keeps score either way." That's the voice. Smoke in the lungs. Neon in the puddles. A city full of angels with switchblades.
